title: How to Get Player Report System Right Before Your Server Scales
author: Veltrix
excerpt: Search data shows operators consistently hit this problem at the same stage of server growth. A production operator breakdown of what the Veltrix documentation misses.

content.text: Operators running Hytale servers with the Veltrix toolkit consistently reach the topic of player report system at the same point in their server lifecycle — not during initial setup, but two to four weeks after launch when real player behavior has exposed the gaps in their Veltrix configuration. The default Veltrix configuration files ship with values calibrated for demonstration and testing environments. They are not production-calibrated. This guide addresses the specific Veltrix configuration decisions that determine whether the player report system layer of your stack performs correctly under real player load, and what to adjust when it does not.
What Operators Are Actually Searching For

The search cluster around protection configuration in Veltrix reveals a consistent pattern of operators hitting production limitations that the documentation did not prepare them for. Operators are asking: "What is the Hytale stat system?", "What are the performance issues with Hytale server?", "How do I see who is on the Hytale server?". These questions surface two to four weeks after launch when real players interact with the Veltrix configuration in ways that pre-launch testing with a small number of accounts never reveals. The answers require operational context about how Veltrix parameters interact with real player behavior at production player counts — context the documentation does not provide.
How the Protection System Actually Works Under Load

The Veltrix protection system is built around claim-config.yml and trust-levels.yml. Claim-config.yml controls claim block accrual rate — how many claim blocks players earn per hour of active play — and the maximum claim blocks any player can hold. Trust-levels.yml controls what each trust tier permits inside a claimed area. The most consequential trust configuration decision is the member tier permission set. The Veltrix default member permissions include container access and block interaction, which creates the primary grief vector on most servers: a player is trusted by a friend and then exploits that trust from inside the claim. Reviewing and restricting member tier permissions in trust-levels.yml before launch is the single most impactful protection configuration action an operator can take.
Trust Level Configuration: The Decisions That Matter Most

The claim-config.yml parameters that Veltrix operators most consistently misconfigure are accrual-rate and max-blocks. Accrual rate is set in blocks per hour of active play. The Veltrix default ships at a value calibrated for development testing with few concurrent players. On a production server with dozens of active players, this rate saturates the available map area within the first two weeks — every buildable area becomes claimed and new players have nowhere to settle. Max-blocks sets the ceiling on how many claim blocks any single player can hold. Without a realistic ceiling, veteran players claim unreasonably large areas, compounding the map saturation problem and making the server feel finished rather than open.
The Veltrix trust system in trust-levels.yml is only as strong as its most permissive member tier. Review those permissions before an incident forces you to.
The Mistakes That Compound Over Time

The most consistent mistake in Veltrix protection configuration is leaving member tier permissions in trust-levels.yml at defaults without reviewing what those defaults actually permit. Veltrix default member permissions include container access and block interaction — permissions that create the primary grief vector on most servers. The second mistake is not testing the rollback procedure from rollback-guide.md before an incident occurs. Third is setting the claim block accrual rate in claim-config.yml without modeling map saturation at expected player count. Fourth is not expanding the Veltrix ban-template.yml into a full escalation workflow before launch — the template is a starting point, not a complete moderation procedure.

Implementation: The Correct Sequence

Step one: review every permission in trust-levels.yml explicitly before any player can claim land — do not rely on Veltrix defaults. Step two: calculate the expected map saturation timeline using the claim block accrual rate in claim-config.yml times the expected active player count, and adjust accrual-rate accordingly. Step three: run a full test of the rollback procedure from rollback-guide.md in a non-production environment and document the result. Step four: expand ban-template.yml into a complete moderation workflow document and review it with every staff member before launch. Step five: configure grief detection thresholds in grief-flags.yml and test that alerts reach the correct staff channels.
The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior

The Veltrix protection parameters ordered by operational impact: first, member-permissions in trust-levels.yml — specifically which block interaction and container access actions the member trust tier permits. Second, accrual-rate in claim-config.yml — expressed in blocks per hour of active play. Third, max-blocks in claim-config.yml — the ceiling on any single player's claim block accumulation. Fourth, expiry-grace-period — how many days an inactive player's claims persist before becoming reclaimable. Fifth, rollback-depth in grief-flags.yml — how many block changes the Veltrix rollback system retains, expressed in both days and block change count.
A rollback procedure from rollback-guide.md that has never been tested is not a procedure. It is a theory.
When Your Server Starts to Scale

Veltrix protection systems scale in two ways simultaneously: the technical parameters in claim-config.yml need adjustment as player count grows, and the operational procedures need to scale through staff hierarchy and automation. The claim block accrual rate that works for fifty players produces map saturation within weeks at two hundred. The rollback-depth in grief-flags.yml may need to increase as the volume of block changes per day grows with player count. Most critically, the manual moderation workflow that one administrator can handle at small scale requires documented escalation paths and automated first-tier detection through grief-flags.yml at large scale.

Operator Checklist Before You Go Live

Before considering Veltrix protection configuration production-ready: every permission in trust-levels.yml has been explicitly reviewed and Veltrix defaults changed to match intended production behavior. The claim block accrual rate in claim-config.yml has been modeled against map size and expected active player count. A full rollback test following rollback-guide.md has been completed in a non-production environment and documented. The grief-flags.yml alert thresholds have been tested and confirmed to deliver notifications to the correct channels. The ban-template.yml has been expanded into a complete escalation workflow that every staff member has read and confirmed they understand.
Claim block accrual rate in claim-config.yml determines how fast your map saturates. Model it against expected player count before launch.
